definition: Define a family of interchangeable algorithms, encapsulate each one in its own class, and make them swappable at runtime.
ruleOfThumb: When you find yourself writing a switch statement to pick between algorithms, those branches want to be classes.

citation.author: Gamma, Helm, Johnson & Vlissides (GoF)
citation.bookTitle: Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software

questions.prompt: The team needs to add a new discount type next sprint. What's the most painful part of the current design?

options.text: Each new discount type means opening and editing this method — the calculation logic for all discount types is tangled together instead of being separate, swappable units
options.explanation: Every new discount type requires modifying existing, working code. There's no way to add a 'loyalty' discount without touching a method that already correctly handles percentage, flat, and bogo discounts — risking regressions in all of them.

options.text: The magic numbers (0.9, 10) should be extracted to named constants
options.explanation: Extracting constants is a good cleanup, but it doesn't change the structural problem. With named constants, you'd still have to open this method and add another branch for every new discount type.

options.text: discountType should be a TypeScript union type or enum instead of a plain string
options.explanation: Stronger typing would catch invalid values at compile time, which is an improvement. But it still leaves the core issue: all discount logic lives in one method, and adding a new type means editing existing code.

options.text: The method should be split into calculatePercentage(), calculateFlat(), and calculateBogo() on the same class
options.explanation: Private helpers reduce the length of calculate(), but the conditional dispatch still lives there. The class still grows with every new discount type — the structure hasn't fundamentally changed.

questions.prompt: This sorting service works fine today. What assumption built into its design will hurt the team when a new algorithm is needed?

options.text: The class assumes the full set of sorting algorithms is known upfront — adding a new one requires modifying this class even though the sorting logic itself hasn't changed
options.explanation: Adding heapsort requires: updating the union type, adding a case to the switch, and adding a private method — all inside a class that was already working. The three existing algorithms have to absorb the risk of a change that has nothing to do with them.

options.text: The switch statement will throw a runtime error if a new value is passed that isn't in the union type
options.explanation: TypeScript's exhaustiveness checking would actually catch this at compile time. But that's a safety net for the symptom — the problem is that adding any new algorithm requires modifying the existing class at all.

options.text: All three sort implementations are private, making them impossible to unit test independently
options.explanation: Testing private methods is a valid concern, but it's a consequence of the structure, not the root issue. If each algorithm were its own class, they'd be naturally testable — and the switch statement would disappear too.

options.text: DataSorter should be a standalone function rather than a class
options.explanation: Whether it's a class or function doesn't change the structural problem — a function with the same switch logic has the same issue. The problem is how new algorithms are added, not the wrapping syntax.

questions.prompt: The product manager wants to add three more shipping carriers next quarter without touching existing carrier logic. Which design makes that possible?

options.text: Version A — each new carrier requires editing the calculate() method, putting FedEx, UPS, and USPS logic at risk every time a new carrier is added
options.explanation: Version A grows with every new carrier: open the method, add a branch, risk a regression in the existing three. Version B adds a carrier by creating a new class that implements ShippingStrategy — the existing calculator and existing strategies are never touched.

options.text: Version B — the interface adds indirection that makes simple carrier rate logic unnecessarily complex
options.explanation: The interface feels like overhead for three carriers. But the question specifies three more carriers next quarter — at six carriers, Version A's calculate() is a maintenance liability. Version B scales to any number of carriers without ever reopening the calculator.
